title: Variables & Lists
subtitle: name your data
explain:
A variable is just a name pointing at a value: `score = 100` means "whenever I say score, I mean 100". A list holds several values in order: `nums = [3, 1, 4]`.

You grab items by position, and positions count FROM ZERO. So `nums[0]` is 3, `nums[1]` is 1, and `nums[-1]` is the last item, 4. `len(nums)` tells you how many items there are (3 here). `nums.append(5)` sticks 5 on the end.

Why this matters: almost every competitive programming problem hands you a list of numbers or a string and asks you to walk through it. If indexing is not automatic for you, every problem above this one gets harder than it should be. This is exactly task 1 to 4 of your day0 worksheet.

Common mistake: thinking `nums[1]` is the first item. It is the SECOND. The first item is `nums[0]`. Off-by-one indexing errors are the single most common beginner bug, and judges give you zero partial credit for them.
example:
nums = [10, 20, 30]
print(nums[0])    # 10  (first item, index ZERO)
print(nums[-1])   # 30  (last item)
print(len(nums))  # 3
nums.append(40)
print(nums)       # [10, 20, 30, 40]
exercise: Type four lines of Python: (1) create a list called nums containing 3, 1, 4 in that order, (2) set a variable first to its first item using indexing, (3) set a variable last to its last item using NEGATIVE indexing, (4) append 5 to nums. Then on a fifth line, write as a comment what print(nums) would now show.
solution:
nums = [3, 1, 4]
first = nums[0]
last = nums[-1]
nums.append(5)
# print(nums) shows: [3, 1, 4, 5]
checklist: Without looking: what index does the FIRST item of a list have?
What does nums[-1] give you, and why is it useful?
Does append return a new list or change the existing one?
interview: Examiner tests whether indexing is automatic, not memorized. Be ready to state instantly that the first item is nums[0] and the last is nums[-1], and that len(nums) is the count not the last index. The builder nuance: off-by-one is the single most common beginner bug and judges give zero partial credit, so when reading code aloud point at each index and say the position. If asked, append mutates the list in place and returns None.

title: Loops & If
subtitle: repeat and filter
explain:
A for loop visits every item in a list, one at a time. `for x in [10, 20, 30]:` runs the indented block three times, with x being 10, then 20, then 30. The indentation (4 spaces) is not decoration: it is how Python knows what is inside the loop. An `if` runs code only when its condition is true.

Two patterns you will use in literally every problem:

1. Accumulate: start `total = 0` BEFORE the loop, then add to it inside the loop.
2. Filter: start with an empty list, loop, and `append` only the items that pass an `if` test. The classic test: `x % 2 == 0` means x is even (divides by 2 with no remainder).

These are tasks 5 and 6 of your day0 worksheet, and they are the skeleton of two pointers, sliding window, everything.

Common mistake: putting `total = 0` INSIDE the loop. That resets your sum on every step, so you end with just the last item. Initialize before, update inside.
example:
data = [2, 4, 6, 8]
total = 0
for x in data:
    total = total + x
print(total)  # 20

mixed = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
evens = []
for x in mixed:
    if x % 2 == 0:
        evens.append(x)
print(evens)  # [2, 4, 6]
exercise: Without looking at the example, write a loop that builds a list called odds containing only the ODD numbers from mixed = [7, 2, 9, 4, 11]. Start from the line mixed = [7, 2, 9, 4, 11] and write the rest yourself (empty list, for, if, append). Then write as a comment what print(odds) shows.
checklist: Why must total = 0 come before the loop, not inside it?
What does x % 2 == 0 actually compute, in plain words?
What happens if you forget to indent the line under for?
interview: Examiner probes the two skeletons under every pattern: accumulate (total = 0 before the loop, add inside) and filter (empty list, loop, append on an if). Be ready to explain WHY total = 0 must sit before the loop: putting it inside resets the sum each step so you end with only the last item. The nuance only the builder knows: x % 2 == 0 is the even test (remainder zero), and Python uses indentation, not braces, to mark what is inside the loop.

title: Strings & Slicing
subtitle: lists of characters
explain:
A string behaves like a list of characters. With `word = "exun"`: `word[0]` is "e", `word[-1]` is "n", and `len(word)` is 4. Slicing cuts out a piece: `word[1:]` means "everything from position 1 onward", which is "xun". `word[1:3]` is "xu" (start included, end excluded). Methods like `word.upper()` give you a transformed COPY: "EXUN".

Why this matters: half of all easy contest problems are string problems. AtCoder A problems love "take the first letter", "reverse this", "is character i equal to character j". Valid Palindrome, which you will hit in the two pointers branch, is pure string indexing.

This is task 7 of your day0 worksheet.

Common mistake: trying to change a string in place. `word[0] = "E"` crashes, because strings are immutable (unchangeable). You never edit a string; you build a new one, for example `"E" + word[1:]`. If you need heavy editing, convert to a list of characters first with `list(word)`.
example:
word = "exun"
print(word[0])          # e
print(word[-1])         # n
print(word[1:])         # xun
print(word.upper())     # EXUN
print("E" + word[1:])   # Exun  (new string, original untouched)
exercise:
Trace this code in your head and type the exact output, one line per print:

word = "stack"
print(word[0])
print(word[-1])
print(word[1:4])
print(word[2:].upper())
solution:
s
k
tac
ACK
checklist: In word[1:4], is the character at index 4 included or excluded?
Why does word[0] = "x" crash, and what do you do instead?
What does word[-1] give for any non-empty string?
interview: Examiner checks that you treat a string as an indexable, sliceable, but IMMUTABLE list of characters. Be ready to explain slicing: word[1:3] includes start, excludes end. The builder nuance and classic gotcha: word[0] = 'X' crashes because strings cannot be edited in place; you build a new one with concatenation ('X' + word[1:]) or convert to list(word) for heavy editing. Methods like upper() return a COPY and leave the original untouched.

title: Dicts & Sets
subtitle: instant lookup
explain:
A dict stores pairs: a KEY you look things up by, and its VALUE. `ages = {"kevin": 14}`, then `ages["kevin"]` gives 14, `ages["bob"] = 16` adds a new pair, and `"kevin" in ages` asks whether a key exists. A set is like a list that ignores duplicates and is instant to search: `seen = set()`, `seen.add(x)`, `x in seen`.

Here is the entire reason these exist in competitive programming: asking `x in some_list` makes Python scan the whole list, item by item. That is O(n) per lookup. Asking `x in some_set` or `key in some_dict` is O(1), effectively instant, no matter how big it is. Swapping a list for a set is often the difference between a solution that passes and one that times out.

This is tasks 8 and 10 of your day0 worksheet, and it is the foundation of the whole hash map branch.

Common mistake: reading a key that is not there. `ages["stuart"]` crashes with KeyError if stuart was never added. Check with `in` first, or use `ages.get("stuart")` which returns None instead of crashing.
example:
seen = set()
seen.add(3)
seen.add(7)
print(3 in seen)   # True
print(5 in seen)   # False

ages = {"kevin": 14}
ages["bob"] = 16
print(ages["kevin"])      # 14
print("stuart" in ages)   # False
exercise: Write the function has_duplicate(items) that returns True if any value appears twice in the list, using a set. Plan: make an empty set called seen; loop over items; if the item is already in seen, return True; otherwise add it; after the loop return False. Type the full function (about 6 lines).
checklist: Why is x in a_set fast but x in a_list slow?
What happens when you read a dict key that does not exist?
In has_duplicate, why does return False come AFTER the loop, not inside it?
interview: Examiner wants the complexity reason these exist: x in a_list is O(n) (scans every item) but x in a_set or key in a_dict is O(1) effectively instant, regardless of size. Be ready to say that swapping a list for a set is often the line between passing and timing out. The builder nuance: a dict stores key->value pairs and reading a missing key raises KeyError, so guard with `in` first or use .get() which returns None. This set/dict idea is the entire foundation of the hash map branch.

title: Functions
subtitle: return, not print
explain:
A function is reusable code with a name. It takes inputs (parameters) and RETURNS an output:

```
def add_one(x):
    return x + 1
```

Now `add_one(5)` is 6. The `return` line is how the answer comes out; the moment Python hits `return`, the function is done and hands the value back to whoever called it.

Why this matters for you specifically: every LeetCode and NeetCode problem is "fill in this function". The judge calls your function with hidden inputs and compares the RETURNED value to the expected answer. Your day1 practice files work the same way: the asserts at the bottom call your function and check what comes back.

This is task 9 of your day0 worksheet.

Common mistake: printing instead of returning. `print(x * 2)` shows the answer on YOUR screen but returns None to the judge, so every test fails even though the output "looks right". Inside a function, the answer must travel through return. Print is for humans; return is for code.
exercise: Write a function count_evens(nums) that RETURNS how many even numbers are in the list. Do not print anything inside it. It must make count_evens([1, 2, 3, 4, 6]) equal 3 and count_evens([1, 3]) equal 0. Type the full function.
checklist: Why does a function that prints the right answer still fail every judge test?
What does a function return if you never write a return line?
What happens to the rest of a function after return runs?
interview: Examiner tests the return-vs-print distinction because it is how every LeetCode/NeetCode problem and your day1 asserts are graded: the judge calls your function and compares the RETURNED value. Be ready to explain the classic gotcha plainly: print(answer) shows it on your screen but returns None to the grader, so every hidden test fails even though the output looks right. The builder nuance: the moment Python hits return the function ends and hands the value back. Print is for humans, return is for code.

title: Contest I/O
subtitle: input, split, map
explain:
AtCoder and HackerRank do not call a function for you. They feed your program lines of text on standard input, and you print the answer. Three tools handle 95 percent of it:

1. `input()` reads ONE line, always as a STRING.
2. `.split()` chops a line on spaces: `"3 5".split()` gives `["3", "5"]`.
3. `map(int, ...)` converts each piece to a number.

So the standard opener for a line like `3 5` is:
`a, b = map(int, input().split())`

For "first line is n, second line is n numbers": `n = int(input())` then `nums = list(map(int, input().split()))`.

Output is just `print(answer)`. One AtCoder trap: answers like Yes and No must match EXACTLY, capital Y, lowercase es. Your own abc185_b solution ends with `print("Yes" if ok else "No")` for exactly this reason.

Common mistake: forgetting input() returns a string. `input() + input()` on lines 3 and 5 gives "35", not 8. Convert with int() before doing math, every single time.
example:
# input:
# 3 5
a, b = map(int, input().split())
print(a + b)        # 8

# input:
# 4
# 10 20 30 40
n = int(input())
nums = list(map(int, input().split()))
print(len(nums))    # 4
print(max(nums))    # 40
exercise: Write a complete program (no function needed) that reads two lines: the first line is a number n, the second line is n space-separated integers. Print their sum. For input lines "4" and "2 7 1 5" it must print 15. Type all three lines.
solution:
n = int(input())
nums = list(map(int, input().split()))
print(sum(nums))
checklist: What type does input() always return, even when the user types 42?
Write from memory the one-liner that reads two integers from one line.
Why would printing YES instead of Yes get a wrong-answer verdict on AtCoder?
interview: Examiner checks contest-style stdin handling, since AtCoder and HackerRank feed text lines instead of calling a function. Be ready to write from muscle memory: a, b = map(int, input().split()) for one line of ints, and n = int(input()) then list(map(int, input().split())) for a count line plus a values line. The builder nuance and gotcha: input() always returns a STRING, so input() + input() concatenates ('35') instead of adding; convert with int() every time. AtCoder also matches output EXACTLY, so 'Yes' not 'YES' (your abc185_b ends with print('Yes' if ok else 'No') for exactly this).

title: Two Sum
subtitle: have I seen this
explain:
The hash map pattern in one sentence: a dict or set answers "have I seen this before?" in O(1), so you can replace a nested double loop, O(n squared), with one single pass, O(n).

Two Sum: given a list and a target, return the INDICES of the two numbers that add to target. The slow way checks every pair. The pattern way keeps a dict mapping value to the index where you saw it. For each number n at index i, ask one question: is `target - n` already in my dict? If yes, you are done: the partner was seen earlier and its index is stored. If no, store n at i and move on.

Why one pass is enough: by the time you reach the SECOND number of the winning pair, the first one is already waiting in the dict. You never need to look forward, only backward.

You already submitted this one for the assignment. Now it has to come out of your fingers from memory, because the live shortlist round will not let you peek at your Google Doc.

Common mistake: inserting the current number into the dict BEFORE checking for its partner. With target 10 and number 5, the 5 then finds itself and you return the same index twice. Check first, insert after.
exercise: From memory, write two_sum(nums, target) that returns the two indices as a list. It must pass: two_sum([2, 7, 11, 15], 9) == [0, 1], two_sum([3, 2, 4], 6) == [1, 2], two_sum([3, 3], 6) == [0, 1]. Check for the partner BEFORE storing the current number.
checklist: Explain why one pass works: where is the first number of the pair when you reach the second?
Why must you check the dict before inserting the current number?
What is the time complexity, and what was it for the nested-loop version?
interview: Pattern: Arrays/Hashing. One-sentence core idea: a dict answers 'have I seen this before?' in O(1), so a nested double loop O(n^2) collapses to one pass O(n). Be ready to explain why one pass suffices: by the time you reach the second number of the winning pair, the first is already stored, so you only look backward. The classic gotcha the builder must own out loud: check whether target - n is in the dict BEFORE inserting the current number, otherwise with target 10 and value 5 the 5 matches itself and you return the same index twice. Store value->index because you must return positions.

title: Group Anagrams
subtitle: fingerprint keys
explain:
This is the hash map pattern one level up: instead of using the value itself as the key, you use a computed FINGERPRINT of the value.

Problem: group anagrams together. ["eat","tea","tan","ate","nat","bat"] becomes [["eat","tea","ate"], ["tan","nat"], ["bat"]].

Key insight: two words are anagrams exactly when they are equal after sorting their letters. sorted("eat") and sorted("tea") are both ['a','e','t']. So the sorted word is a perfect dict key: every word joins the list stored under its own fingerprint, and anagrams collide into the same bucket automatically.

The shape: dict from fingerprint to list of words. Loop once, compute `"".join(sorted(w))` for each word, append the word to that key's list, and finally return `list(d.values())`.

This fingerprint idea generalizes hard: count signatures, normalized forms, any "these things belong together if some computed key matches".

Common mistake: using sorted(w) directly as the key. sorted returns a LIST, and lists cannot be dict keys (unhashable type error). Join it into a string first with "".join(sorted(w)), or convert to a tuple. Bonus tool: d.setdefault(key, []).append(w) saves you the "if key not in d" dance.
exercise: From memory, write group_anagrams(strs) that returns the groups as a list of lists. For ["eat","tea","tan","ate","nat","bat"] it must produce the three groups (any order). Use the sorted-string fingerprint as the dict key.
checklist: Why are two words anagrams exactly when their sorted letters match?
Why does sorted(w) fail as a dict key, and what are the two fixes?
What does d.setdefault(key, []).append(w) do when the key is new? When it already exists?
interview: Pattern: hash map one level up, using a computed FINGERPRINT as the key instead of the raw value. One-sentence core idea: two words are anagrams exactly when their sorted letters match, so the sorted string is a perfect dict key and anagrams collide into the same bucket automatically. Complexity is O(n*k log k) for n words of length k, versus comparing every pair. The classic gotcha: sorted(w) returns a LIST which is unhashable and cannot be a dict key, so join it into a string with ''.join(sorted(w)) (or use a tuple). Builder tip: d.setdefault(key, []).append(w) skips the if-key-not-in-d dance.

title: Valid Palindrome
subtitle: meet in the middle
explain:
Two pointers in one sentence: instead of nested loops comparing everything with everything, you keep two index variables and walk them toward each other (or along together), doing one comparison per step.

Valid Palindrome is the cleanest example. A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards ("racecar"). Put a left pointer l at index 0 and a right pointer r at the last index. Compare s[l] and s[r]. If they differ, it is not a palindrome, return False immediately. If they match, move l up one and r down one, and repeat while l < r. If the pointers meet without ever disagreeing, return True.

Why this beats building a reversed copy: same O(n) time, but the pointer version uses no extra memory and, more importantly, the l and r dance is the EXACT skeleton you reuse in Two Sum II next. Learn the skeleton here where the logic is easy.

The full LeetCode version also skips punctuation and ignores case; the skeleton stays identical, you just add "while the character is not alphanumeric, skip it" before each comparison.

Common mistake: writing the loop condition as l <= r or forgetting to move BOTH pointers, which either does a useless self-comparison or loops forever. Condition l < r, and both pointers move every round.
example:
def is_palindrome(s):
    l = 0
    r = len(s) - 1
    while l < r:
        if s[l] != s[r]:
            return False
        l = l + 1
        r = r - 1
    return True

print(is_palindrome("racecar"))  # True
print(is_palindrome("exun"))     # False
exercise: Trace is_palindrome("abcba") by hand. Write one line per loop round in the form: l=?, r=?, s[l]=?, s[r]=?, match yes/no. Then write the final returned value.
solution:
l=0, r=4, s[l]=a, s[r]=a, match yes
l=1, r=3, s[l]=b, s[r]=b, match yes
l=2, r=2, loop stops because l < r is False
returns True
checklist: Why is the loop condition l < r and not l <= r?
What is the very first thing you do when the two characters differ?
How would you extend this to ignore punctuation without changing the skeleton?
interview: Pattern: Two Pointers. One-sentence core idea: keep a left pointer at index 0 and a right pointer at the last index, compare, and walk them toward each other, one comparison per step while l < r. Complexity is O(n) time and O(1) space, beating the build-a-reversed-copy approach on memory, but the real reason to learn it here is that the l/r skeleton is reused verbatim in Two Sum II. The classic gotcha: the loop condition is l < r (not <=) and BOTH pointers must move every round, or you do a useless self-comparison or loop forever. The full LeetCode version just adds 'skip non-alphanumeric, lowercase' before each compare; the skeleton is unchanged.

title: Two Sum II
subtitle: sorted, shrink ends
explain:
Same question as Two Sum (find two numbers adding to target) but the input list is SORTED, and that one fact unlocks a completely different solution with zero extra memory.

Put l at the start and r at the end, and look at the sum s = nums[l] + nums[r]:

1. s == target: found it.
2. s too SMALL: you need a bigger sum. The only move that can increase it is l = l + 1, because nums[l] is the smallest thing in play.
3. s too BIG: you need a smaller sum, so r = r - 1.

Every step permanently rules out one element, so the whole thing is one pass, O(n), no dict needed. This is THE classic demonstration of why "sorted" in a problem statement should make your two-pointer alarm ring.

Compare with hash map Two Sum: that one works on UNSORTED input but pays O(n) memory for the dict. Sorted input lets you trade the dict for pointer logic. Knowing which variant fits which problem is exactly what pattern drilling is for.

Common mistake: this LeetCode problem wants 1-INDEXED positions (first element is position 1), so you return [l + 1, r + 1]. Returning plain [l, r] fails every test even with perfect logic. Always re-read what exactly the problem wants returned.
exercise: From memory, write two_sum_sorted(nums, target) returning the 1-indexed positions as a list. It must pass: two_sum_sorted([2, 7, 11, 15], 9) == [1, 2] and two_sum_sorted([1, 3, 4, 8], 12) == [3, 4]. Use the l and r pointer skeleton, no dict.
checklist: When the sum is too small, why is moving l the ONLY move that can help?
What property of the input makes this work, and what breaks if the list is unsorted?
Hash map Two Sum vs pointer Two Sum II: what does each cost in memory?
interview: Pattern: Two Pointers on a SORTED array. One-sentence core idea: with l at the start and r at the end, if the sum is too small move l up (only way to increase, since nums[l] is the smallest in play), if too big move r down. Complexity O(n) time and O(1) space, versus hash-map Two Sum which works on unsorted input but pays O(n) memory for the dict; be ready to contrast the two and say 'the word sorted should make my two-pointer alarm ring'. The classic gotcha: this problem wants 1-INDEXED positions, so return [l + 1, r + 1]; returning [l, r] fails every test with otherwise perfect logic.

title: Valid Parentheses
subtitle: LIFO matching
explain:
A stack is a pile: you add to the top and remove from the top. Last In, First Out (LIFO). In Python a plain list IS a stack: `stack.append(x)` pushes, `stack.pop()` removes and returns the top item.

Valid Parentheses: given a string like "({[]})", decide if every bracket closes in the right order. Why a stack fits perfectly: the most recently opened bracket must be the FIRST one to close. "Most recent first" is literally the definition of LIFO.

The algorithm: walk the string. Opening bracket: push it. Closing bracket: the top of the stack must be its matching opener; pop it and compare. Mismatch, or popping when the stack is empty (a closer with nothing open), means invalid. After the loop, the stack must be EMPTY: leftover items mean brackets that were opened and never closed, like "((".

A tidy trick: a dict mapping each closer to its opener, `pairs = {")": "(", "]": "[", "}": "{"}`, turns the matching test into one lookup.

Common mistake: forgetting the final empty check and returning True after the loop unconditionally. "(" would then pass, because the loop itself never finds a mismatch. The bug hides until a test case has unclosed openers.
example:
stack = []
stack.append("a")
stack.append("b")
stack.append("c")
print(stack.pop())  # c  (last in, first out)
print(stack.pop())  # b
print(stack)        # ['a']
exercise: From memory, write is_valid(s) for a string containing only ()[]{}. It must pass: is_valid("({[]})") == True, is_valid("(]") == False, is_valid("(") == False, is_valid("") == True. Use a list as the stack and a dict mapping closers to openers.
checklist: Why does LIFO match how brackets nest? Say it in one sentence.
Why must the stack be empty at the end, and what input breaks the code if you skip that check?
What two situations make a closing bracket invalid the moment you see it?
interview: Pattern: Stack (LIFO). One-sentence core idea: the most recently opened bracket must be the first to close, which is literally Last-In-First-Out, so push openers and on a closer pop and check it matches. Complexity O(n) time and O(n) space. The classic gotcha the examiner loves: you must also check the stack is EMPTY at the end, otherwise '((' passes because the loop alone never finds a mismatch. Two ways a closer is invalid the instant you see it: the stack is empty (closer with nothing open) or the popped top is the wrong opener. Builder tip: a dict {')':'(', ']':'[', '}':'{'} turns matching into one lookup.

title: Daily Temperatures
subtitle: monotonic stack
explain:
Problem: for each day's temperature, how many days until a warmer one? [73,74,75,71,69,72,76,73] gives [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0]. The brute force scans forward from every day: O(n squared).

The monotonic stack idea: keep a stack of INDICES of days that are still WAITING for a warmer day. As you walk the list, each new temperature answers the waiters it beats: while the new temp is warmer than the temp at the index on top of the stack, pop that index, and its answer is (today's index minus that index). Then push today, because today is now waiting too.

Why it is called monotonic: the temperatures at the stacked indices always run hottest at the bottom to coolest on top. Anything that would break that order gets popped and resolved first.

Why it is O(n) even with the inner while loop: every index is pushed exactly once and popped at most once, so the total work across the whole run is 2n operations.

This pattern answers any "next greater element" question, which is a contest regular.

Common mistake: pushing TEMPERATURES instead of INDICES. When the pop happens, you need the index to compute how many days the waiter waited; the temperature alone cannot tell you that. Stack the index, look up the temperature via temps[index].
example:
temps = [73, 74, 75, 71]
# i=0: stack empty, push 0           stack: [0]
# i=1: 74 > temps[0]=73, pop 0, ans[0] = 1-0 = 1, push 1
# i=2: 75 > temps[1]=74, pop 1, ans[1] = 2-1 = 1, push 2
# i=3: 71 < temps[2]=75, just push 3 stack: [2, 3]
# days 2 and 3 never get answered, stay 0
# result: [1, 1, 0, 0]
exercise: From memory, write daily_temperatures(temps) returning the list of wait times. It must pass: daily_temperatures([73,74,75,71,69,72,76,73]) == [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0]. Start with ans = [0] * len(temps), stack the indices, pop inside a while loop.
checklist: Why store indices on the stack instead of temperatures?
Why is the total cost O(n) even though there is a while loop inside the for loop?
What do the indices still sitting on the stack at the end mean, and what answer do they get?
interview: Pattern: monotonic stack for 'next greater element'. One-sentence core idea: keep a stack of INDICES of days still waiting for a warmer day; each new temperature pops and answers every waiter it beats, then pushes itself. Complexity is O(n), not O(n^2), and be ready to justify it: each index is pushed once and popped at most once, so total work is about 2n even with the inner while loop. The classic gotcha: store INDICES not temperatures, because when you pop you need the index to compute days waited (today minus that index); look the temperature up via temps[index]. Indices left on the stack at the end never found a warmer day, so they keep answer 0.

title: Buy/Sell Stock
subtitle: track the minimum
explain:
Problem: prices[i] is a stock price on day i. Buy on one day, sell on a LATER day, maximize profit. [7,1,5,3,6,4] gives 5 (buy at 1, sell at 6). If no profit is possible, answer 0.

The brute force tries every buy day with every later sell day: O(n squared). The one-pass insight: walking left to right, the best possible deal ending TODAY is today's price minus the CHEAPEST price seen so far. So carry just two variables: min_so_far and best profit. Each day, first try selling (update best with price minus min_so_far), then update min_so_far if today is a new low.

This is your entry into sliding window thinking: a window with the buy day at the left edge and the sell day at the right edge, where you slide the right edge one day at a time and keep only a tiny summary (the minimum) instead of re-scanning the window. The full grow-and-shrink machinery arrives in Longest Substring next; this problem teaches the "maintain window state cheaply" half.

Common mistake: answering max(prices) minus min(prices). That ignores ORDER: in [9, 1] it claims profit 8, but the 9 happens before the 1, and you cannot sell before you buy. Tracking min_so_far as you walk is what enforces buy-before-sell.
example:
prices = [7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4]
# day 7: min=7, best=0
# day 1: min=1, best=0
# day 5: 5-1=4, best=4
# day 3: best stays 4
# day 6: 6-1=5, best=5
# day 4: best stays 5
# answer: 5
exercise: From memory, write max_profit(prices) in one pass with two variables. It must pass: max_profit([7,1,5,3,6,4]) == 5 and max_profit([7,6,4,3,1]) == 0. No nested loops.
checklist: Why is max(prices) - min(prices) wrong? Give the two-element counterexample.
What exactly do the two carried variables mean at any point mid-walk?
Why does one pass suffice when brute force needed two nested loops?
interview: Pattern: Sliding Window, the 'maintain window state cheaply' half. One-sentence core idea: walking left to right, the best deal ending today is today's price minus the cheapest price seen so far, so carry just two variables, min_so_far and best. Complexity O(n) one pass versus O(n^2) trying every buy/sell pair. The classic gotcha to call out: the answer is NOT max(prices) - min(prices), because that ignores order; in [9, 1] it claims profit 8 but you cannot sell before you buy. Tracking min_so_far as you walk is exactly what enforces buy-before-sell.

title: Longest Substring
subtitle: grow right, shrink left
explain:
Problem: length of the longest substring without repeating characters. "abcabcbb" gives 3 ("abc").

This is the full sliding window machine. The window is s[l..r], and you keep one invariant: NO duplicates inside the window. Track the window's contents in a set.

The loop: move r forward one character at a time (grow right). If s[r] is already in the set, the invariant broke, so shrink from the left: remove s[l] from the set and do l = l + 1, repeating WHILE the duplicate is still inside. Once the window is clean again, its size (r - l + 1) is a candidate answer.

Why this is O(n) and not quadratic: l only ever moves forward. Across the entire run, r takes n steps and l takes at most n steps, so the total is at most 2n, even though there is a while inside the for. Same argument as the monotonic stack, and it is worth being able to SAY that argument out loud.

Common mistake: on finding a duplicate, resetting the whole window (l = r) or restarting the scan from l + 1. Restarting re-reads the same characters over and over and lands you back at O(n squared), which is exactly what the window exists to avoid. Shrink one step at a time, only as far as needed.
example:
s = "abcabcbb"
# r=0 'a': window "a",   best=1
# r=1 'b': window "ab",  best=2
# r=2 'c': window "abc", best=3
# r=3 'a': duplicate! drop s[l]='a', l=1, window "bca", best=3
# r=4 'b': duplicate! drop 'b', l=2, window "cab", best=3
# ... best never beats 3, answer: 3
exercise: From memory, write length_of_longest(s) using a set and the l pointer. It must pass: length_of_longest("abcabcbb") == 3, length_of_longest("bbbbb") == 1, length_of_longest("") == 0.
checklist: State the window invariant in one sentence without looking.
Why is this O(n) despite a while loop inside the for loop?
Why is resetting l all the way to r on a duplicate both wrong-ish and slow?
interview: Pattern: Sliding Window, the full grow-right shrink-left machine. One-sentence core idea: hold the invariant 'no duplicate inside the window', grow r forward, and when s[r] duplicates, shrink from the left (remove s[l], l += 1) until the window is clean, tracking the max size r - l + 1. Complexity is O(n): be ready to say l only ever moves forward, so r and l each take at most n steps, about 2n total despite the inner while. The classic gotcha: on a duplicate do NOT reset l all the way to r or restart the scan from l+1, because re-reading characters drops you back to O(n^2), which is the whole thing the window exists to avoid.

title: AtCoder A/B Habits
subtitle: 5-minute A problems
explain:
AtCoder Beginner Contest A problems are 2 to 5 lines: read input, one formula or check, print. B problems add a loop or a simple simulation. You already have 28 accepted solutions from ABC 181 to 200; this node is about converting "submitted once" into "can produce on demand".

The drill, straight from your plan: re-solve YOUR OWN submissions blind. Open a problem at atcoder.jp (URL pattern: atcoder.jp/contests/abc181/tasks/abc181_a), write it without looking at your old code, submit, then compare. Targets: A problems under 5 minutes, B problems 5 to 15 minutes.

The habits that make those targets reachable:

1. Read constraints FIRST. They tell you whether a loop is fine or you need a pattern.
2. Type the I/O line before thinking: `map(int, input().split())` should be muscle memory by now.
3. Match output EXACTLY: Yes not YES, and mind float formatting.
4. Test against ALL the provided samples before submitting. Your abc185_b was verified against all 5 official samples before it ever touched the judge.

Common mistake (you have the scar already): a wrong-answer verdict from submitting the wrong clipboard or a pasted note line causing a compile error. Slow is smooth: re-read what you pasted before hitting submit.
example:
# ABC193 A, your accepted solution, 2 lines:
# price A discounted to B, print the discount percentage
a, b = map(int, input().split())
print((a - b) / a * 100)

# input  "100 80"  ->  20.0
# AtCoder accepts it because the allowed
# error is 0.01, so 20.0 matches 20
exercise: Blind re-solve, A-problem style: a shop sells an item at price A, now discounted to price B (two integers, one input line). Write the complete 2-line program that prints the discount as a percentage of A. For input "100 80" it prints 20.0. No peeking at your atcoder folder.
solution:
a, b = map(int, input().split())
print((a - b) / a * 100)
checklist: What are your time targets for A and for B problems?
What two things do you check before hitting submit on AtCoder?
Why re-solve problems you already got accepted, instead of doing new ones?
interview: This node is about converting 'submitted once' into 'can produce on demand' for the live round. The drill: blind re-solve your own ABC 181-200 A/B submissions, A under 5 min, B 5-15 min. Be ready to name the four pre-submit habits: read constraints FIRST (they say whether a plain loop is fine or you need a pattern), type the map(int, input().split()) line from muscle memory, match output EXACTLY (Yes not YES, mind float formatting where AtCoder allows 0.01 error), and test against ALL provided samples before submitting. The builder scar to mention: a wrong-answer verdict from a pasted note line or wrong clipboard, so slow is smooth, re-read what you pasted.

title: Timed Virtual Contest
subtitle: A+B in 20 minutes
explain:
The Exun shortlist round is a LIVE contest. Knowing the patterns is necessary but not sufficient; you also need to perform them with a clock running. This node is the final level of your ladder: one ABC virtual contest, problems A and B, in 20 minutes.

How to run it: on atcoder.jp, pick an ABC you have NOT re-solved recently, start a virtual participation (or just a phone timer), and solve A then B as if it counts. Submit to the real judge so verdicts are honest.

Rules of engagement, from your plan:

1. 10-minute stuck rule: if a problem has eaten 10 minutes with no progress, note where you got stuck and move on. In a live round, a stubborn B costs you an easy A.
2. After time is up, write ONE line per problem naming the idea ("hash map of seen values", "monotonic stack"). If you cannot name the idea, you have not extracted the lesson.
3. If you read an editorial afterward, close it and re-code from memory. Reading is not the same as being able to produce it.

Common mistake: treating a failed timed run as a verdict on you instead of data. The point of a virtual contest is to find which pattern is slow under pressure, then drill exactly that one. One run per session, then back up the tree to whichever node was shaky.
example:
# 20-minute session template
# 0:00  open ABC xxx problem A, read constraints
# 0:0x  A accepted -> open B
# 10:00 stuck-check: progress on B? if no, note blocker
# 20:00 stop. write idea lines:
#   A: "string indexing"
#   B: "simulate with running minimum"
# then: re-drill the node that felt slow
exercise: Speed drill, A-problem style, aim for under 5 minutes: read one line containing a 3-digit string S (for example "447") and print the sum of its three digits (15 here). Write the complete program, treating each character with int().
solution:
s = input()
print(int(s[0]) + int(s[1]) + int(s[2]))
checklist: What does the 10-minute stuck rule say you must do, exactly?
After reading an editorial, what must happen before the problem counts as learned?
What do you do with the result of a timed run, pass or fail?
loophole: The assignment EXPLICITLY allows reading editorials, so the honest way to use that: attempt alone for 30 minutes, read the editorial, then CLOSE it and re-type the solution from memory, and write one line on the core idea ('hash map of seen values', 'monotonic stack'). Shortlisters can smell pasted code that the writer can't explain, so the UNDERSTANDING is the real deliverable, not the characters. Time tactic for the AtCoder batches: do every contest's A problem first (under 5 minutes each), then circle back for the B's, so one slow B never eats the whole evening.
interview: This is the live-contest performance layer; patterns are necessary but not sufficient with a clock running. The workflow nuance examiners cannot fake: editorials are EXPLICITLY allowed, so the honest method is attempt alone for 30 min, read the editorial, then CLOSE it and re-code from memory plus a one-line idea note ('hash map of seen values', 'monotonic stack'), because the understanding is the real deliverable and shortlisters can smell pasted code the writer cannot explain. Time tactic for the batches: do every contest's easy A first (under 5 min each), then circle back for the B's, so one stubborn B never eats the evening. Rules: 10-minute stuck rule (note the blocker and move on), and treat a failed timed run as data pointing at the slow pattern, not a verdict on you.

title: Binary Search
subtitle: halve the search space
explain:
Linear scan is O(n): you look at every element. If the data is SORTED, you can do better. Binary search keeps two pointers, lo and hi, looks at the middle, and throws away half the range every step. n elements take about log2(n) steps: a million elements in 20 looks, a billion in 30.

The loop invariant is the whole trick: the answer, if it exists, is always inside [lo, hi]. While lo <= hi, look at mid = (lo + hi) // 2. If nums[mid] == target you are done. If nums[mid] < target the answer is to the right, so lo = mid + 1. If it is bigger, hi = mid - 1. The two `+1 / -1` are not decoration: they shrink the range so the loop must end.

The pattern that wins contests is "binary search on the answer". When a problem asks for the smallest / largest value that still works, and "works" is monotonic (once it works it keeps working), you binary-search over the answer itself instead of an array. That is one of the four patterns you are drilling for the Exun shortlist.

Classic infinite loop: writing `lo = mid` instead of `lo = mid + 1`. If mid never moves past lo, the range stops shrinking and the loop hangs. Always step past mid.
exercise: From memory, write bsearch(nums, target) on an ascending list, returning the index or -1. It must pass: bsearch([1, 3, 5, 7, 9], 7) == 3, bsearch([1, 3, 5, 7, 9], 4) == -1, bsearch([2], 2) == 0, bsearch([], 5) == -1. Use lo <= hi and remember the +1 / -1.
checklist: Why does the range have to shrink every iteration, and which line guarantees it?
How many steps for a billion sorted elements, roughly, and why log2?
What does 'binary search on the answer' need to be true about the problem (the monotonic property)?
interview: Secondary node. Core idea: on SORTED data, look at the middle and throw away half each step, so n elements take about log2(n) looks (a billion in 30). The contest-winning variant is 'binary search on the answer' when 'works' is monotonic. Classic gotcha to mention if asked: write lo = mid + 1, not lo = mid, or the range stops shrinking and the loop hangs.

title: Prefix Sums
subtitle: range sums in O(1)
explain:
A contest gives you an array and then asks for the sum between index l and r, a hundred thousand times. Summing the slice each time is O(n) per query, so O(n*q) total, and you time out. Prefix sums turn each query into one subtraction, O(1).

Build one array once: pre[0] = 0, and pre[i] = pre[i-1] + nums[i-1]. So pre[i] is the sum of the FIRST i elements. Then the sum of nums[l..r] (inclusive) is just pre[r+1] - pre[l]. You added everything up to r+1 and subtracted everything before l, leaving exactly the window.

Why the extra leading zero and the r+1: the shifted-by-one indexing is what makes the boundary math clean and lets l = 0 work without a special case. Draw it once on paper with [2, 4, 1, 3] and the off-by-one stops being scary.

This is the same precompute-once-answer-many idea behind a lot of harder problems (2-D prefix sums for grids, difference arrays for range updates). Get the 1-D version into muscle memory first.
example:
# precompute, then every range sum is one subtraction
def build_prefix(nums):
    pre = [0] * (len(nums) + 1)
    for i, x in enumerate(nums):
        pre[i + 1] = pre[i] + x
    return pre

pre = build_prefix([2, 4, 1, 3, 5])
# sum of index 1..3 (4 + 1 + 3 = 8):
print(pre[4] - pre[1])   # 8
exercise: Write range_sum(nums, queries) where queries is a list of (l, r) inclusive pairs; return a list of the sums, using a prefix array (build it ONCE, not per query). It must pass: range_sum([2, 4, 1, 3, 5], [(0, 2), (1, 3), (4, 4)]) == [7, 8, 5].
checklist: Why is pre one element longer than nums, and what does pre[0] = 0 buy you?
Derive sum(l..r) = pre[r+1] - pre[l] in words.
What was the time complexity before (per query and total) versus after?
interview: Secondary node. Core idea: precompute pre[i] = sum of the first i elements once, then any range sum nums[l..r] is the single subtraction pre[r+1] - pre[l], turning O(n) per query into O(1). The nuance: the leading pre[0] = 0 and the r+1 shift make the boundary math clean and let l = 0 work with no special case.

title: Recursion & Backtracking
subtitle: try, recurse, undo
explain:
A recursive function solves a problem by calling itself on a smaller version. Two parts, always: the BASE CASE that stops (no smaller version left), and the RECURSIVE CASE that does one step and hands the rest to itself. Miss the base case and you blow the stack.

Backtracking is recursion for "generate all the ways" problems: subsets, permutations, placing queens, sudoku. The shape is choose / explore / un-choose. You make a choice, recurse to build on it, then UNDO the choice before trying the next one, so the path is clean for the sibling branch. The undo is the part beginners forget, and then every branch leaks state into the next.

Think of it as walking a tree of decisions. Going down = choose. Hitting a leaf = record a complete answer. Coming back up = un-choose. The same skeleton solves a whole family of problems once you see it.

It is also the honest way to understand DP later: DP is backtracking plus a memo so you stop re-solving the same subproblem. Learn the slow, correct recursion first; speed comes after.
example:
# all subsets of a list, via choose / explore / un-choose
def subsets(nums):
    out, path = [], []
    def dfs(i):
        if i == len(nums):
            out.append(path[:])      # leaf: record a copy
            return
        path.append(nums[i])         # choose
        dfs(i + 1)                   # explore with it
        path.pop()                   # un-choose
        dfs(i + 1)                   # explore without it
    dfs(0)
    return out
# subsets([1, 2]) -> [[1, 2], [1], [2], []]
exercise: Write permutations(nums) returning every ordering, using backtracking (a used set or swap, then undo). It must pass: sorted(permutations([1, 2, 3])) has 6 lists and includes [1, 2, 3] and [3, 2, 1]. Remember to undo your choice before the next branch.
checklist: Name the two parts every recursion needs, and what happens if the base case is missing.
In backtracking, why must you undo the choice before the next branch?
Why append path[:] (a copy) at a leaf instead of path itself?

title: Graphs: BFS & DFS
subtitle: explore a grid or network
explain:
Once you can recurse, graphs open up. A graph is just nodes and edges, stored as an adjacency list: a dict mapping each node to its neighbours. A grid is a graph too, where each cell's neighbours are up / down / left / right.

Two ways to walk it, and one shared rule: keep a `visited` set so you never process a node twice (that is what stops infinite loops on cycles). DFS goes deep first, easiest as recursion or a stack. BFS goes level by level using a QUEUE (collections.deque), and that level-by-level order is special: on an unweighted graph, the first time BFS reaches a node, it reached it by the SHORTEST path. So DFS for "can I reach / connected components", BFS for "fewest steps".

The bread-and-butter problem is counting connected components, also seen as "number of islands": loop over every cell, and each time you find an unvisited land cell, run one flood-fill from it and count it as a new island. The flood-fill marks the whole island visited so you do not recount it.

Gotcha: mark a node visited when you ENQUEUE it in BFS, not when you pop it, or the same node gets added to the queue many times before you ever process it.
exercise: Write count_islands(grid), a list of lists of 0s and 1s; return how many groups of 1s are connected horizontally or vertically. Use BFS or DFS flood-fill with a visited set. It must pass: grid [[1,1,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,1]] -> 2.
checklist: Why does BFS find shortest paths on an unweighted graph but DFS does not?
Why mark a node visited when you enqueue it rather than when you pop it?
What is the role of the outer double loop in the islands problem versus the inner flood-fill?

title: DP: 1-D Tables
subtitle: remember subproblems
explain:
Dynamic programming sounds scary and is not. It is recursion where subproblems OVERLAP, so instead of re-solving the same thing a thousand times you solve each subproblem once and write the answer in a table.

Climbing stairs is the cleanest first example: to reach step n you came from step n-1 or step n-2, so ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2). The naive recursion recomputes ways(3) over and over and is exponential. The fix is either memoize (cache results in a dict) or build a table bottom-up: dp[0] and dp[1] are the base cases, then fill dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] up to n. O(n) time, and only the last two values matter so you can even drop to O(1) space.

The recipe for any 1-D DP: (1) define dp[i] in one sentence as a quantity, (2) write the recurrence that builds dp[i] from smaller indices, (3) set the base cases, (4) decide the fill order so a cell's dependencies are ready before it. Coin change, house robber, longest increasing subsequence all fit this mould.

The mistake is starting to code before you can say in plain words what dp[i] MEANS. Define the meaning first; the recurrence falls out of the definition.
exercise: Write coin_change(coins, amount): the FEWEST coins that sum to amount, or -1 if impossible. Use a dp table where dp[a] = min coins for amount a, dp[0] = 0. It must pass: coin_change([1, 2, 5], 11) == 3, coin_change([2], 3) == -1, coin_change([1], 0) == 0.
checklist: State in one sentence what dp[i] means in coin change before writing any code.
Why is the naive stairs recursion exponential, and what exactly does the table remove?
What decides the fill order of a DP table?
